turns.text: Hello Ashleigh, thank you so much for taking the time to speak with me today. I'm a researcher studying maternity care experiences, and I'm really interested in hearing your personal story. I understand you're in the early stages of pregnancy, and I'd love to hear about your experience in your own words. There are no right or wrong answers — I'm just here to listen and learn from you. Everything you share will help us understand how to make maternity care better. Shall we begin?

*shifts in chair, tucking a strand of hair behind ear*

Yeah, okay. Thank you for... for asking me about this. Honestly, it's kind of a relief to talk to someone who's actually listening.

*pauses, looking down at hands*

I'm still getting used to saying it out loud, you know? I'm pregnant. At thirty-eight, after years of wondering if it would even happen. And I'm terrified. Not in the way I expected — it's not just the normal stuff. It's... I feel so alone in this, even though my husband's supportive. He's working so much just to make ends meet that sometimes I'm sitting here by myself, Googling things I probably shouldn't be Googling at two in the morning.

The medical side has been confusing. I went in thinking they'd want to do all these tests because of my age, but they keep saying I'm "low-risk," like that's supposed to make me feel better. But I worry about things — molar pregnancies, ectopic pregnancies — stuff I'd never even heard of before. And when I try to bring it up, I feel like they're... not dismissing me exactly, but rushing through. Like my anxiety doesn't matter because statistically I'm fine.

*looks up*

The hardest part is the distance. We're out here, and the specialist clinic is far. The cost of gas, time off work — it all adds up so fast. I just want someone to tell me my baby is okay and actually *hear* me when I say I'm scared.
Imagine you've just decided with your partner — or on your own — that you'd like to start trying for a baby. Walk me through what that moment felt like and what your very first steps were.

*takes a deep breath, eyes getting a little watery*

Oh man. Okay, so... we'd been talking about it for years, honestly. Just talking. There was always a reason to wait — finances, my job situation, you know. And then one night, maybe six months ago, we were just lying in bed and my husband said, "What if we stopped waiting? What if we just... tried?" And I remember my heart doing this thing where it jumped and sank at the same time.

*fidgets with a coffee mug on the table*

I was terrified. I'm thirty-eight. I'd read enough to know that every year matters at this age. But there was also this feeling like — if not now, when? We can't afford to wait anymore in some ways. So we just... decided. No big plan. We weren't going to obsess over it.

*laughs, a bit hollow*

Except I immediately obsessed over it. My first step was basically going down an internet rabbit hole. I bought prenatal vitamins, started tracking my cycle on my phone — which, by the way, is way more complicated than I thought. I was trying to figure out when I was ovulating, reading about basal body temperature, all of it.

And then I called my OB's office to make an appointment, just for a pre-conception check. That took three weeks to get in. When I finally went, I felt so unprepared, like I should have had more of a plan. The doctor was nice enough, but the visit was quick. She said I looked healthy and wished me luck.

*looks at you*

And then... I got pregnant the second month. Which sounds lucky, and I guess it is, but it also terrified me more.

Imagine you've booked a preconception appointment with your GP and you're sitting in the waiting room. What's going through your mind as you wait, and what are you hoping will happen in that consultation?

*shifts uncomfortably, crossing arms*

Oh God, the waiting room. I remember sitting there thinking I was probably wasting their time. Like, I'm not sick, nothing's wrong with me — why am I here? But also, I'd spent so much time getting this appointment that I was determined to make it count.

*looks around nervously*

I had this whole list in my purse. I'd written down questions the night before — stuff about my family history, whether I should change anything about my diet or exercise, if there were tests I should get because of my age. I kept checking the list over and over, worried I'd forget something important. There was this older woman across from me with her daughter, and I wondered if she'd had her kids young and if it was easier. Like, was I screwing myself by waiting this long?

*voice gets quieter*

What I was really hoping for... I needed someone to tell me it was going to be okay. That my age wasn't going to be some massive obstacle. And I wanted them to actually *know* my situation — not just look at a chart and say "you're healthy." I needed them to understand that I live out here, that money is tight, that I'm anxious about this.

*pauses*

But honestly? I was also braced for disappointment. I've learned not to expect much. I figured they'd spend five minutes with me, say everything looks fine, and send me on my way. Which is basically what happened.

Imagine you've been trying to conceive for several months without success. A close friend suggests you try a fertility app she found helpful. How do you react to that suggestion, and what do you do next?

*laughs a bit bitterly, then catches herself*

Actually, I already had one. The second month we decided to try, I downloaded like three different apps. I was obsessed with them — tracking everything, checking my fertile window down to the hour.

*leans back in chair*

But your question... if a friend had suggested it after months of nothing? I think I would've felt this weird mix of things. Grateful that she was thinking of me, but also kind of... ashamed? Like, it would've felt like admitting that my body wasn't doing what it's supposed to do. Even though logically I know that's not how it works.

*fidgets with hands*

I probably would've downloaded it immediately though. That's what I do — I research, I try things, I convince myself that if I just *do* enough, control enough, maybe it'll work. The apps feel like I'm taking action, you know? Like I'm not just sitting around waiting.

*voice becomes more hesitant*

But I also know I would've spiraled a little. Gone into that place where you start wondering if there's something wrong with you. At thirty-eight, that clock in your head gets louder every month you don't see a positive test. I probably would've asked her a million questions about whether she had trouble too, what her doctor said, the whole thing.

*looks down*

Lucky for me, I got pregnant in the second month. But those conversations with friends — when they're trying to help but also accidentally reminding you that time's running out — they hurt more than people realize.

Imagine you've just had a difficult conversation with your partner about whether you're both truly ready to have a child — financially, emotionally, practically. What does 'being ready' actually mean to you, and who or what helped you form that picture?

*pauses for a long time, staring out the window*

Ready. God, that word haunts me.

*turns back*

The honest answer? I don't think we were ready. I'm still not sure we are. But I also don't think you ever *are* ready in the way you imagine. I had this picture in my head from... I don't know, magazines, Instagram, people talking — this idea that you'd have a house paid off, a solid savings account, a partner with a stable job, and then you'd decide "now is the time." Very planned. Very controlled.

*voice gets tighter*

That's not us. My husband and I had that conversation, and it was hard. He was worried about money — we still are. I was worried about everything. But what I realized is that being ready, for me, doesn't mean having it all figured out. It means being willing to figure it out *as you go*. It means wanting it enough to be scared.

*rubs face*

Who shaped that for me? Honestly, a lot of it came from my mom. She had me young, wasn't "ready" by any measure, and she made it work anyway. She'd say things like "you just do it" — not dismissively, but like... you find a way because you have to. Because you want to.

*looks directly at you*

But I also think I formed this picture from fear, if I'm being real. At thirty-eight, the alternative to not being ready was potentially never being a mother. So "ready" became... flexible.

Imagine you've experienced a previous pregnancy loss and are now considering trying again. You're sitting with that knowledge as you think about the future. What thoughts and feelings come up, and what would you need from a healthcare team to feel supported going forward?

*stops, looks confused*

Wait — I haven't had a loss. This is my first pregnancy. I think you might be mixing up my situation with someone else's?

*pauses, then softens*

But honestly, talking about it... I'm terrified of loss. That's different, I know. I haven't *experienced* it, but I live with this constant fear that something will go wrong. The not knowing is almost worse sometimes than actually going through something, because at least then you'd know what you're dealing with.

*wraps arms around self*

I think that's part of why I'm so anxious right now. I don't have a history of loss to point to and say "see, this happened to me." Instead, I have this vague terror about all the things that *could* happen. Molar pregnancies, miscarriages, ectopic pregnancies — all these medical disasters I never knew existed until I got pregnant.

*voice becomes quieter*

If I were someone who'd lost a pregnancy, though? I think I'd need my healthcare team to actually acknowledge that grief. To not act like a loss is just a statistical blip. And I'd need them to listen to my fears without rushing me through appointments or making me feel like I'm being dramatic.

*looks at you*

Right now, what I need is someone who hears how scared I am — not because of past loss, but because this matters so much to me. Does that make sense?

Imagine you have a pre-existing health condition and you're wondering how it might affect a future pregnancy. You decide to look it up before speaking to anyone medical. What do you search for, and how does what you find make you feel?

*shifts uncomfortably*

I don't have a pre-existing condition, actually. My health is pretty straightforward — that's one of the few things I'm not worried about, medically speaking.

*pauses, then adds quietly*

Though honestly, I do the searching thing anyway. Even without a condition. I'll read about gestational diabetes, preeclampsia, all these complications that can happen in pregnancy. And every time I find something new, I convince myself that's what's going to happen to me.

*rubs temples*

It's like... my brain searches for problems. I'll have a cramp and Google "early pregnancy complications" and suddenly I'm reading about placental abruption or something terrifying, and I'm convinced that's me. The internet is both my best friend and my worst enemy right now.

*looks up*

I think if I actually *had* a condition — diabetes, high blood pressure, something real — I'd probably be even worse about it. I'd search obsessively for stories of other women with the same thing who had healthy pregnancies, but I'd also find the horror stories. And then I'd be too scared to tell anyone I was that scared, because I'd feel like they'd already warned me and I'd just be confirming their worst fears about me.

*voice gets smaller*

That's actually part of why I haven't brought up some of my bigger worries with my doctor. I'm afraid if I say them out loud, they'll become real.
Imagine it's the morning after you've taken a positive pregnancy test. Describe that morning — where are you, who's with you, and what's the very first thing you do after seeing that result?

*eyes get bright, almost teary*

Oh God. That morning.

*takes a shaky breath*

I woke up at like five-thirty, before my husband. I just... knew. I can't explain it. I had this feeling in my body that was different. So I got up, went to the bathroom — quietly, trying not to wake him — and I took the test. I had three of them under the sink, just in case.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

I sat on the edge of the bathtub and watched those two lines appear. And I just... I froze. I couldn't move. I kept staring at it, thinking maybe I was reading it wrong. I held it up to the light, turned it different ways. My hands were shaking so bad.

*pauses, voice cracks slightly*

The first thing I did was cry. Just sat there crying — happy crying, terrified crying, all mixed together. Then I went back to bed and just... lay there next to my husband, holding the test, not knowing how to tell him. It felt too big to say out loud. Like if I said it, something might break it.

*wipes eyes*

I finally woke him up and just handed it to him without saying anything. He looked at it, then at me, and he started crying too. We just held each other in the dark for like twenty minutes without talking.

*looks down*

And then the fear started creeping in. Which sounds crazy, but that's when I realized how real it was. How much I had to lose.
When you decided who to tell first — or whether to tell anyone at all — what was behind that decision? What felt safe or risky about sharing the news?

*leans forward, speaking more carefully*

We told basically no one for the first eight weeks. That was deliberate.

*fidgets with the edge of the table*

Everyone says you're supposed to wait until after the first trimester, right? And I get it now — the statistics, the miscarriage risk, all that. But for us, it wasn't just about being cautious. It was about protecting something that felt impossibly fragile.

*voice gets quieter*

If I told people too early and then something went wrong, I'd have to un-tell them. I'd have to say "never mind, there's no baby." And I couldn't... I couldn't handle that. It felt like if I kept it secret, kept it just mine and my husband's, I could keep it safe somehow. Like the fewer people who knew, the less real the possibility of losing it became.

*pauses*

There was also shame mixed in there, I think. Like, I'm thirty-eight, this is my first pregnancy — if something went wrong, I didn't want people looking at me with pity. Or worse, like I'd done something wrong. I wanted to know the baby was okay before I had to answer a million questions and worried looks.

*looks at you*

My mom kept asking why I was distant on the phone, and I almost broke. But I held it together. When I finally told her at nine weeks, after I heard the heartbeat, she cried. She said she'd been praying.

*voice softens*

I think I needed that certainty first. I needed proof it was real before I let other people love it too.

Imagine you're at your 20-week anomaly scan. The sonographer goes quiet for a moment and says they need to take a closer look at something. What goes through your mind in that silence, and how do you respond in the room?

*face goes pale, grips the edge of the table*

Oh God. I haven't had that scan yet — I'm still in the first trimester. But just... imagining that?

*voice becomes shaky*

That silence would kill me. That moment where they go from routine to "something's not quite right" — I think that's every pregnant woman's worst nightmare. My stomach just dropped thinking about it.

*takes a breath, trying to steady herself*

I think I'd freeze. Like, physically freeze. My first instinct would be to look at my husband, to see if he heard what I heard. Then I'd probably start trying to read the sonographer's face, looking for clues. Are they concerned? Do they see something bad? And my mind would immediately go to the worst-case scenarios — chromosomal issues, heart defects, something that means this baby won't survive.

*voice gets smaller*

I'd probably ask questions but in this weird, detached way. Like I'm not really in my body anymore. "Is something wrong? Is the baby okay?" But I'd be braced for the answer to be no. I'd be preparing myself.

*looks down*

And honestly? The fear would probably come out as anger or numbness rather than crying. I'd go into survival mode. I'd want them to tell me exactly what they're seeing, what it means, what happens next — because at least then I'd have information. The not knowing would be worse than bad news.

*pauses*

I'm already anxious about that scan and I haven't even gotten there yet.
In that moment, how much did you feel like you could ask questions or speak up — or did something stop you? What shaped that?

*leans back, considering the question carefully*

I think... I'd want to ask questions, but I'm not sure I actually would. Not fully, anyway.

*twists hands together*

There's this thing that happens to me in medical settings. I get really small. Like, I know the doctors and nurses have expertise I don't have, so I defer to them. But it's more than that — it's like I don't want to seem difficult or high-maintenance. Especially as a "low-risk" patient, you know? I don't want them to think I'm one of those anxious first-time moms who's going to be a pain.

*voice gets quieter*

A lot of it comes from how I grew up, I think. My family wasn't big on questioning authority. You went to the doctor, they told you what was wrong, and you did what they said. You didn't ask a lot of questions because that felt rude or like you were doubting them.

*pauses, looking uncomfortable*

And there's also the money thing. We're paying out of pocket for a lot of this. The gas to get there, the time off work — it all costs. So when I'm in that room, there's this underlying anxiety that if I ask too much, take up too much time, maybe they'll think I'm not a "good" patient. Stupid, I know.

*meets your eyes*

But honestly? In that moment with the sonographer going quiet? I think fear would override all of that. I'd probably find my voice then, even if it shook. Because this is my baby. That might be the one time I'd actually speak up.

Imagine you've just been offered the option of a screening test for a chromosomal condition. The midwife explains the test briefly and asks if you'd like to go ahead. How does that conversation feel, and how do you make your decision?

*shifts in seat, looking overwhelmed*

Okay, so that hasn't happened yet for me. I'm still early — like, nine weeks or so. But I know those tests are coming, and honestly, I'm already dreading it.

*rubs face*

That conversation would stress me out so much. The midwife explains it in what, five minutes? And then I'm supposed to make this decision that could change everything, and I don't want to sound stupid asking for clarification. What exactly are we testing for? What do the results mean? What happens if something shows up? If I say no, am I putting my baby at risk by not knowing?

*voice gets tighter*

I think I'd say yes to whatever test they recommend, because I'd be too scared to say no. But I'd hate that I didn't really understand it. I'd go home and spend hours on the internet trying to figure out what I just agreed to, reading about false positives and what different risk levels mean.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

The hard part is... I don't know what I'd actually *do* with the information. If they found something, would I want to know? Would it change anything? And I feel like I should have strong opinions about that going in, but I don't. I'm just scared.

*looks at you*

I wish someone would sit down with me — really sit down, not rush — and help me understand what these tests are for, what the options are. Not just tell me what to do, but actually help me think through it.

Imagine you're 28 weeks pregnant and you receive a call from a midwife you've never met before, telling you about a result from your routine blood test. This is the third different midwife you've spoken to this pregnancy. How does that feel, and what does it mean for how you receive that information?

*exhales heavily, shaking head*

God, that would be so frustrating. Three different midwives? I'd feel like I'm starting over every single time.

*voice becomes strained*

Each one would need me to repeat my whole story — where I live, my work situation, my anxieties. And they wouldn't know any of that context. They'd just see a chart. So when this new midwife calls about a blood test result, I'd immediately feel like... defensive? Like, do they even know me well enough to be telling me something important about my baby?

*leans forward*

And honestly, getting a call like that out of the blue would terrify me. A result from a routine test — that phrasing alone would send my mind racing. Is something wrong? Why are they calling instead of waiting for my next appointment? The fact that it's a stranger delivering the news makes it worse, not better.

*voice gets quieter, more vulnerable*

I'd probably ask a lot of questions that a midwife I'd built a relationship with wouldn't have to field. Because I wouldn't trust that they actually understood what I needed to hear or how I needed to hear it. And I'd be frustrated that I have to explain myself again when I'm already anxious.

*looks at you directly*

That's been one of the hardest parts of this whole thing — never seeing the same person twice. It makes me feel like I don't matter, like I'm just a file they're processing. Not a person carrying a baby who's terrified and needs some consistency.

Imagine it's 2am and you're 32 weeks pregnant. You've noticed something that worries you — reduced fetal movements. You're not sure whether to call the hospital or wait until morning. Walk me through what you're thinking and what you do.

*eyes go wide, voice urgent*

Oh God, two in the morning with reduced movements? I wouldn't sleep. I just wouldn't.

*stands up, starts pacing*

My first thought would be panic — immediate panic. Is something wrong with the baby? Is this serious? And then I'd start second-guessing myself. Am I overreacting? Maybe the baby's just sleeping. Maybe I'm being paranoid again.

*runs hand through hair*

But then I'd think about how out here, we're far from the hospital. If something *is* wrong and I wait until morning, what if that time matters? What if waiting costs me my baby? That thought would override everything else.

*sits back down, speaking faster*

I'd probably lie there for an hour trying to convince myself it's fine. I'd poke at my belly, try to get the baby to move. I'd drink some juice, eat something, change positions. All the things you read about. But if nothing changed, I'd wake my husband up, even though he has work in a few hours. I'd be like, "Something's different. I need to call."

*voice becomes quieter, more anxious*

And then I'd be terrified to call because what if they think I'm crazy? What if I'm the anxious first-time mom crying wolf at two in the morning? But I also couldn't live with myself if I didn't call and something happened.

*looks at you directly*

I'd call. I'd have to. Even if I felt stupid doing it. This baby is too important.

Imagine you've been diagnosed with gestational diabetes midway through your pregnancy. Your midwife hands you a leaflet and refers you to a dietitian. Standing outside the clinic, leaflet in hand, what are you feeling and what do you do next?

*clutches imaginary leaflet, face crumples slightly*

Oh my God. That would... that would devastate me.

*voice shakes*

Because gestational diabetes is one of those things I've already worried about. I've read about it at three in the morning. And now it's real, and it's my fault somehow — like maybe I ate too much sugar, didn't exercise enough, my body failed at this one basic thing.

*starts walking, agitated*

Standing outside the clinic, I think I'd feel numb at first. Then angry. Then guilty. Like, I'm already struggling with money and time, and now I have to see a dietitian? How much is that going to cost? When am I supposed to fit that in with work? And what if the dietitian tells me I can't eat things we can actually afford?

*looks down at hands*

I'd probably go sit in the car and cry for a while. Then I'd call my husband and try to explain it without falling apart. He'd probably say something practical like "we'll figure it out," which would help and also make me feel worse because I'm the one who has to manage this.

*pauses*

The leaflet would go in my bag, and I'd read it obsessively later. I'd search online for gestational diabetes stories — some reassuring, some terrifying. And I'd be so worried about passing this on to my baby, about what it means for him or her.

*voice gets smaller*

Then I'd call the dietitian's office, probably before I even told my parents. Because at least with a professional, I could ask questions without judgment.

Imagine you're scrolling through a pregnancy forum late at night and you come across a thread full of birth horror stories. You can't stop reading. What's drawing you in, and what do you do with those feelings afterwards?

*winces, recognizing herself*

Oh God, yeah. I do that. I definitely do that.

*settles back into chair, speaking more quietly*

It's like... I can't look away. Someone's describing their emergency C-section or their hemorrhage or their baby being in the NICU, and I'm reading every word even though it's terrifying me. My heart's pounding, but I keep scrolling.

*pauses, trying to explain*

I think part of it is that I need to know what *could* happen. Like if I read enough horror stories, I'll somehow be prepared. If I know all the things that go wrong, maybe I can prevent them or at least not be shocked if something happens to me. It's this weird logic where reading scary stuff feels like control.

*voice gets smaller*

And honestly? There's also something about reading other women's stories that makes me feel less alone. Even the bad ones. Like, at least I know I'm not the only one terrified.

*rubs face*

But afterwards... I feel awful. I can't sleep. I lie there thinking about everything that could go wrong during labor, and I spiral. My husband finds me at two in the morning just staring at the ceiling, and he knows what I've been doing. He'll say "you need to stop reading that stuff," and he's right, but I can't seem to stop myself.

*looks at you*

The next day I feel exhausted and anxious for no good reason. I've literally scared myself with worst-case scenarios that probably won't happen. But I keep doing it anyway.
After reading stories like that, did you find yourself raising your concerns with a midwife or doctor — or did you hold onto them quietly? What made you decide either way?

*looks down, ashamed*

I held onto them. Quietly.

*fidgets with hands*

I mean, I tried once or twice to bring things up. Like, I mentioned that I'd read about molar pregnancies and I was worried about it in early pregnancy. And the midwife just... kind of nodded and said "that's very rare" and moved on. She didn't sit with the fear. She just dismissed it with statistics.

*voice becomes quieter*

So after that, I stopped. I learned pretty quickly that my anxieties don't really fit into the appointment structure. They want me to come in, take my blood pressure, ask if I'm having any bleeding or severe pain, and then I'm done. There's no space to say "I'm terrified about things I read on the internet at two in the morning."

*looks up, conflicted*

And honestly? Part of me didn't want to bring it up because I didn't want to be labeled as anxious or high-maintenance. I'm already aware that I'm older, first-time mom, probably seem neurotic. If I start listing all my fears, won't they just... not take me seriously? Or worse, think I'm not capable of handling pregnancy?

*voice cracks slightly*

So I kept quiet. I'd read the horror stories, feel terrified, try to convince myself it won't happen to me, and then move on to the next day. But it builds up, you know? All that fear with nowhere to put it. My husband gets tired of hearing it. My friends don't get it. So it just sits inside me.

*pauses*

I should've spoken up more. I know that now.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes getting watery*

It's just... I feel like there's this expectation that you're supposed to be grateful and calm about pregnancy, especially at my age. Like, "Oh, you're finally pregnant, you should be thrilled, not worried." And I am grateful — I am. But I'm also terrified, and those two things exist at the same time.

*voice gets stronger, more frustrated*

And nobody really wants to hear about the terror part. The doctors see it as a medical problem to manage. My husband sees it as something he can't fix, so he gets frustrated. My mom keeps saying "you're healthy, the baby will be fine," like reassurance is supposed to make the fear go away. It doesn't.

*leans forward*

What I needed was someone to say, "Your fear makes sense. You waited a long time for this. You're living far from resources. You're worried about money. Of course you're anxious. Let's talk about how we can actually support you through that." Not dismiss it, not just give me statistics.

*voice becomes quieter*

Instead, I learned to keep it in. I scroll the forums alone at night. I worry alone during the day. And I show up to appointments and say everything's fine because I don't want to be a burden.

*looks directly at you, eyes shining*

But it's exhausting. Being alone with this much fear while carrying a baby — it's exhausting. And I don't think it's good for either of us, honestly.

*sits in silence for a moment, then speaks more slowly*

I think... I think part of why I'm struggling so much is that I don't feel seen. Not really.

*voice wavers*

When I go to appointments, they see my chart. Thirty-eight, first pregnancy, low-risk, employed. Checkboxes. They don't see me sitting in my car for twenty minutes before I go in, trying to calm down enough to walk through the door. They don't see me calculating whether I can afford gas money for the next visit while my husband's picking up extra shifts. They don't see how isolated I am out here, how much harder everything feels when you're doing it alone in the woods.

*rubs eyes*

And the healthcare system doesn't seem built for someone like me. The appointments are scheduled like everyone has a simple situation. But I'm juggling work, money, distance, age, anxiety — and it all compounds. One missed appointment because I couldn't get time off becomes this whole cascade of worry because now I'm behind where I should be.

*voice gets firmer*

What would actually help? Someone who checks in on how I'm *really* doing, not just whether my baby's heartbeat is normal. Someone who knows my situation well enough to remember it between visits. Someone who believes that my fear matters, even if statistically I'm "low-risk."

*pauses, looking vulnerable*

And maybe... someone who acknowledges that being a first-time mom at thirty-eight, after waiting so long, after all the what-ifs and the worry — that's a lot. That's a lot to carry alone.

*shifts in chair, looking tired but determined to keep talking*

I'm also angry, I think. I haven't really said that out loud yet.

*voice hardens slightly*

I'm angry that I had to wait this long to have a baby. I'm angry that society makes you feel like your worth is tied to motherhood, but then doesn't actually support you when you finally get there. I'm angry that healthcare feels like a luxury I have to budget for. That I have to choose between seeing a specialist and paying for groceries.

*stands up, pacing again*

And I'm angry at myself for being so afraid. Like, I should be stronger than this. I should be able to handle pregnancy without falling apart at two in the morning. Women do this all the time, right? But they probably have partners who aren't working double shifts. They probably live closer to hospitals. They probably don't spend their lunch breaks at work worrying that something's wrong.

*voice cracks*

I'm also grieving, kind of. Even though nothing's wrong — as far as I know — I'm grieving the pregnancy I thought I'd have. The one where I'd be relaxed and glowing and excited. Instead, I'm terrified and isolated and exhausted before I even get to labor.

*sits back down, looking at you directly*

And nobody talks about that. They talk about morning sickness and cravings and nursery design. But not this. Not the fear that eats you alive, or the grief of having the wrong circumstances for something you wanted so badly.

*pauses, voice smaller*

I don't know how to do this and be okay at the same time.

*long pause, staring at hands*

I think I'm also scared I'm going to fail at this. At motherhood.

*voice barely above a whisper*

Like, what if I get to the end of this pregnancy and I'm so exhausted and anxious that I can't be the mother this baby deserves? What if my fear transfers to the baby somehow? What if I'm too old, too tired, too broke to actually do this right?

*looks up, eyes glistening*

I spent so many years waiting for this, imagining it would be this perfect thing. And now that it's happening, I'm realizing how unprepared I am — not just physically, but emotionally. Financially. I worry constantly about whether we should even be doing this. Whether I'm being selfish for wanting a baby when we're struggling so much.

*voice shakes*

My husband tries to reassure me, but I can see the stress in his face. He's working himself to exhaustion to pay for this pregnancy, and I feel guilty about that every single day. What if the baby comes and we still can't afford childcare? What if I have to go back to work and I never see my baby? What if I'm one of those mothers who resents her own child because life got too hard?

*wipes eyes*

And the isolation makes it worse. I don't have a support system out here. No family nearby, no close friends who've been through this. Just me and my husband and this enormous responsibility growing inside me, and we're both terrified and pretending we're not.

*voice becomes even quieter*

Sometimes I wonder if I made a mistake. And then I feel like a monster for thinking that.

*takes several deep breaths, composing herself*

But then... then I feel the baby move, and everything shifts.

*places hand on stomach unconsciously*

It's still early for big movements, but sometimes I feel this flutter. And in those moments, all the fear doesn't disappear, but it gets... quieter. Like, this is real. There's a person in there. And no matter how scared I am, no matter how unprepared we are, this baby is coming.

*voice softens*

And I think about what my mom did — had me young, no money, no plan. She made it work. Not perfectly, but she loved me fiercely. And I turned out okay. Maybe that's enough. Maybe you don't need to have everything figured out to be a good mother.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

But I also don't want to just accept that this has to be this hard. I don't want my daughter — or son, we don't know yet — I don't want my baby to grow up thinking that struggle is just how things are. That healthcare is a luxury. That asking for help makes you weak.

*voice becomes more determined*

I want better for them. Which means I need to ask for better for myself right now, even though it terrifies me. Even though I feel like I'm being difficult or needy.

*looks directly at you*

Someone needs to hear this. Not just me saying it in my kitchen at night, but actually... heard. By the healthcare system, by society, by my doctors. That pregnant women out here, struggling women, anxious women — we matter too. Our fears are valid. We deserve care that actually sees us.

*voice wavers*

I'm trying so hard to believe that. But it's hard when you feel invisible.
Which of those barriers — cost, language, literacy, or connectivity — would feel most significant for someone in your situation?

*doesn't hesitate*

Cost. Absolutely cost.

*leans forward intensely*

Everything else — language, literacy, connectivity — those are real issues for people, I'm sure. But for me, it's money that's strangling everything. It's the barrier underneath all the other barriers.

*counts on fingers*

The gas to get to appointments. That's real money I have to plan for weeks ahead. The time off work — we can't afford for me to lose hours. Prenatal vitamins, even though they're supposed to be standard care, cost money. The specialist clinic is farther away and costs more. Parking, childcare if I had other kids, all of it adds up.

*voice gets frustrated*

And connectivity and literacy — I have those. I have internet, I can read, I understand medical information. But that almost makes it worse because I can research everything and then realize I can't afford half the recommended care anyway. At least if I didn't understand, I wouldn't know what I was missing.

*sits back*

The thing about cost is that it compounds every other problem. Because I'm struggling financially, I can't take time off to see doctors, so I miss appointments or rush through them. Because I'm rushing, I don't ask questions. Because I don't ask questions, I don't get the support I need. Because I don't get support, my anxiety gets worse.

*looks at you earnestly*

If money wasn't a barrier, everything else would be easier to navigate. But as long as I'm choosing between gas and groceries, between work and healthcare... nothing else matters.

*voice becomes more emotional*

And the thing that really gets me is that I'm not even poor by some standards. We're not homeless. We have jobs. We have a house. But we're still terrified all the time about money.

*shakes head*

My husband makes decent money when he works full hours, but then we have to budget it so carefully. One unexpected expense and we're in trouble. An extra specialist visit, a lab fee that wasn't covered, a tank of gas — it throws everything off. And I know there are people in much worse situations than us, so I feel guilty complaining about it.

*voice hardens*

But that guilt is part of the trap, you know? It keeps me quiet. It keeps me from asking for help or advocating for myself because I think, "Well, at least I have something. I shouldn't complain." Meanwhile, I'm stressed enough that it's probably affecting the baby.

*pauses, looking conflicted*

And I'm aware that the farther out we live, the more we chose isolation for other reasons — peace, quiet, space. But those same reasons that make our life beautiful in normal times make healthcare access a nightmare when you're pregnant and terrified. There's no clinic within twenty minutes. The specialists are even farther. It's not like I can just pop in for reassurance when I'm anxious.

*voice becomes quieter but more urgent*

So cost isn't just about money. It's about access. It's about whether the healthcare system is actually designed for people like me — rural, working, stretched thin. And it's not. It's designed for people who can afford to live closer to everything, who have more flexibility in their jobs.

*looks at you*

That's what nobody talks about.

*takes a shaky breath, eyes glistening*

I think what really scares me is that if something goes wrong, the cost of that will be even worse.

*voice trembles*

Like, if I have complications and need emergency care, we're not prepared for that financially. A hospital stay, an emergency C-section, if the baby needs to go to the NICU — I don't even want to think about those bills. We don't have savings for that. We're barely scraping by month to month.

*wraps arms around herself*

So there's this layer of fear underneath everything else. I'm not just worried about whether my baby is healthy. I'm worried about what it will cost us to have a healthy baby. What if we go into debt? What if we lose the house? What if I have to choose between my baby's medical care and our family's financial survival?

*voice cracks*

And I can't talk to anyone about that because it sounds insane. Like, shouldn't the most important thing be that the baby is okay, regardless of cost? And yes, of course. But I also have to live in this reality where I have limited resources and unlimited medical needs right now.

*pauses, looking exhausted*

The healthcare system assumes everyone can just... handle it. That you'll get the care you need and figure out the money later. But what if you can't? What if you're already stretched so thin that a medical emergency would actually break you?

*looks directly at you*

I think about that every single day. And nobody at the clinic asks about it. Nobody says, "How are you managing financially? Are you able to afford the care you need?" They just schedule the next appointment and assume I'll be there.

*sits in heavy silence for a moment, then speaks very quietly*

I think I'm also grieving the version of motherhood I thought I'd have.

*voice becomes almost child-like*

When I was younger, I imagined being pregnant would be this joyful thing. That I'd have a partner who could take time off work, that we'd be financially stable, that I'd be surrounded by family and friends celebrating with me. I'd glow. I'd feel connected to other mothers. I'd have time to prepare, to nest, to actually enjoy it.

*wipes eyes*

Instead, I'm isolated. My husband is exhausted from working so much. I'm terrified most of the time. I can't tell people because I'm not ready to explain my fear to everyone. There's no celebration, just this quiet dread mixed with desperate hope that nobody can really understand unless they've been here.

*voice becomes stronger, almost angry*

And I feel like I'm supposed to just accept that this is how it is. Like, be grateful you're pregnant at all. Stop complaining about the circumstances. Other women have it worse. And maybe that's true, but it doesn't make my experience less real or less hard.

*pauses*

I wanted to be the kind of mother who had energy left over for her baby after taking care of herself. But I'm already so depleted before this baby even arrives. How am I supposed to give them what they need when I'm running on empty?

*looks at you, vulnerable*

Sometimes I wonder if the baby would be better off if we'd waited. If we'd gotten our finances more stable first. If I'd had this at thirty instead of thirty-eight. And then I feel guilty for thinking that.

*voice gets smaller*

I don't know how to want this and be okay at the same time.

Thank you so much for sharing all of that with me. Your experiences and insights are incredibly valuable. Before we finish, is there anything else about your maternity experience that we haven't covered today — anything that feels important to you that you'd like to add?

*pauses, gathering thoughts, looking like she's considering whether to say something*

Yeah, actually. There is.

*takes a deep breath*

I want to say... I'm not ungrateful. I know that sounds weird after everything I've said, but I need that to be clear. I *am* grateful to be pregnant. This is a miracle to me. I waited so long for this.

*voice becomes more steady*

But I also think the healthcare system — and society in general — needs to understand that gratitude and fear aren't mutually exclusive. I can be thrilled about my baby and terrified about everything else at the same time. And both of those things deserve to be taken seriously.

*leans forward*

What I really needed from this experience, what I still need, is for someone to actually *see* me. Not just as a low-risk chart or a checkbox on a form. See me as a person who's been waiting, who's scared, who's isolated, who's struggling with money, who needs more than a five-minute appointment and a leaflet.

*voice gets firmer*

And I want other women like me to know — older first-time moms, rural women, women without a lot of money, anxious women — your fear matters. Your isolation matters. You're not being dramatic or ungrateful for wanting better support.

*pauses, eyes watery*

I just... I needed to say that. Out loud. To someone who's actually listening.

*looks at you*

Thank you for that.
